lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: I will have my revenge ere I depart his house.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: How, my lord, I may be censured, that nature thus gives way

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: to loyalty, something fears me to think of.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: I now perceive, it was not altogether your brother’s evil disposition made

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: him seek his death; but a provoking merit, set a-work by a

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: How malicious is my fortune, that I must repent to be just!

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: This is the letter he spoke of, which approves him an intelligent

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: party to the advantages of France: O heavens! that this treason were

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: not, or not I the detector!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Go with me to the duchess.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: If the matter of this paper be certain, you have mighty business in hand.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: True or false, it hath made thee earl of Gloucester. Seek out

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: where thy father is, that he may be ready for our apprehension.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: If I find him comforting the king, it will stuff his suspicion

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: more fully.—I will persevere in my course of loyalty, though the conflict

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: be sore between that and my blood.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: I will lay trust upon thee; and thou shalt find a dearer

glosses.definition: provoking cause — Cornwall finds Gloucester's 'treachery' cause enough

glosses.phrase: nature thus gives way to loyalty
glosses.definition: Edmund's formula — family feeling surrendered to political allegiance
a conscious agent; an informed accomplice
whatever the fact of the matter

annotations.title: Edmund the informer — the play's most efficient villainy
annotations.body: Edmund betrays Gloucester to Cornwall in nineteen lines. 'This is the letter he spoke of, which approves him an intelligent party to the advantages of France.' Edmund's aside — 'I will persevere in my course of loyalty, though the conflict be sore between that and my blood' — is pure pretense; there is no conflict. Shakespeare lets Edmund's villainy be self-aware without qualm. The play's most chilling figures are not the raging sisters but this calm tactician who has studied how to convert kinship into profit. Edmund is modern in the worst sense: he does not hate his father; he just has a use for his father's blindness.

annotations.title: Nineteen lines that seal Gloucester's blinding
annotations.body: This scene is nineteen lines long and contains the decisive act of denunciation. Edmund hands Cornwall the letter that Gloucester's 'intelligence' reveals; Cornwall promises Edmund the title Earl of Gloucester in return. The compression matters — the play's greatest atrocity (3.7) is set up in fewer than twenty lines of scene-time. Shakespeare is showing how fast betrayal can unfold when the conditions are right: a son ready to sell a father, a duke ready to pay the price, and a document as evidence. Nothing more is required.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

trials.prompt: How does Edmund's betrayal of Gloucester differ in character from the sisters' cruelty to Lear?
options: Edmund is calm, tactical, and profit-driven — not raging like the sisters; the play's most modern villainy is affective indifference, not hatred
Edmund hates Gloucester more than the sisters hate Lear

trials.prompt: Why is 3.5 effective at nineteen lines?
options: Its compression shows how fast betrayal unfolds when conditions align — a son ready to sell his father, a duke ready to pay, a document as evidence; nothing else required
